info.topictitle: Section 1: Monarchies in England and France (1066–1223)

markdown:
## **Overview**


1. When **William the Conqueror** took over England in 1066, he **changed the way the country was run by giving land to his Norman followers and making sure the king had more power than before.**
2. William also ordered the** Domesday Book in 1086,** which was basically a **giant survey of who owned what land and how much tax they should pay.**
3. Later English kings set up **stronger royal courts** and made sure that the king’s laws reached across the country, **so local lords couldn’t act completely on their own.**
4. In France, the** Capetian kings started out weak, **mostly controlling the land around Paris, but **over time they made their rule more secure **by making deals with the Church and passing down the throne to their heirs.
5. Both England and France built up better systems of government in the 1100s, like **keeping written records, sending out officials to check on local areas, and creating stronger royal administrations.**
6. Even so, nobles in both countries still had a lot of power, and** kings often had to deal with rebellions or fights with powerful barons **who didn’t want to lose control.
7. By around 1223, **both monarchies had grown stronger and more organized, **but kings still needed to bargain with nobles if they wanted to keep their authority and avoid trouble.
